scored.player: David Sacks — White House AI & Crypto Czar
scored.rationale: As the president's chief AI advisor, Sacks shapes major executive orders including the AI Action Plan and coordinates policy across the executive branch, giving him enormous agenda-setting power. Interest is high but split between AI and cryptocurrency portfolios, and his part-time SGE status somewhat limits sustained engagement.

As Senate-confirmed OSTP Director (74-25), Kratsios co-authored the AI Action Plan, oversees NIST/CAISI, and drives the administration's push for a uniform federal AI framework—making AI governance the defining priority of his tenure. His full-time, confirmed position gives him more institutional durability than Sacks.

scored.player: Dept. of Commerce / BIS (Howard Lutnick, Secretary)
scored.rationale: Commerce/BIS controls the AI chip export regime—the single most consequential AI governance lever globally—rescinded Biden's AI Diffusion Rule in May 2025, and received a 23% BIS budget increase for FY2026 enforcement. However, AI competes with broader trade, tariffs, and industrial policy priorities for the Secretary's attention.

scored.player: NIST / CAISI (Center for AI Standards and Innovation)
scored.rationale: Renamed from the US AI Safety Institute in June 2025, CAISI develops the widely adopted AI Risk Management Framework, operates the 290+ member AI Safety Institute Consortium, and leads model evaluations—but its frameworks are voluntary and it lacks enforcement authority. AI standards and evaluation is CAISI's entire mission.

scored.player: Federal Trade Commission (FTC) — Chair: Andrew Ferguson
scored.rationale: The FTC has statutory enforcement authority over deceptive AI claims under Section 5 and is enforcing the TAKE IT DOWN Act on AI deepfakes, but Chair Ferguson has explicitly stated the FTC will not regulate AI proactively and set aside the Biden-era Rytr AI consent order as inconsistent with the AI Action Plan. AI is one of several enforcement areas.

scored.player: DOD / CDAO (Chief Digital and AI Office)
scored.rationale: The January 2026 AI Acceleration Strategy mandates vendors deploy latest models within 30 days of release and establishes 7 Pace-Setting Projects, backed by the enormous defense budget. The CDAO has been granted expanded authority over data access, model procurement, and enterprise AI architecture across the entire department.

OMB's April 2025 memos M-25-21 and M-25-22 are binding government-wide directives setting the framework for how every federal agency uses and procures AI, making it the gatekeeper for federal AI governance. However, AI is one of many OMB responsibilities alongside the full federal budget and regulatory review portfolio.

scored.player: DOJ AI Litigation Task Force — AG Pam Bondi
scored.rationale: Established January 9, 2026, with the mission of challenging state AI laws deemed inconsistent with federal policy through litigation—a powerful deterrent effect even before lawsuits are filed. However, the task force is brand new, its constitutional authority is untested, and legal challenges will take years to resolve.

Approximately 70% of DARPA's programs now incorporate AI/ML, with major initiatives including AI Forward, SABER (battlefield AI red-teaming), and CLARA (high-assurance AI)—giving it outsized influence on defense AI R&D and AI safety evaluation science. However, DARPA's role is upstream R&D rather than governance policy-making.

The December 2025 EO directs Carr to initiate a proceeding on federal AI reporting/disclosure standards, but experts widely doubt the FCC has legal authority over AI, and FCC Commissioner Gomez has publicly questioned this authority. AI is an emerging but peripheral assignment for a telecommunications-focused agency.

CISA has an AI Roadmap, published AI data security guidance with international partners (May 2025), and leads AI risk assessments for critical infrastructure sectors—but is operating under budget pressure and its AI governance role is limited to cybersecurity and critical infrastructure protection.

NSF invests over $700 million annually in AI, announced $100 million for AI Research Institutes (July 2025), and operates the NAIRR pilot connecting 600+ research teams—making it the primary federal funder of non-defense AI research. However, its influence is through research funding rather than governance or regulation.

The State Department represents the U.S. in international AI governance forums (UN, OECD, G7) and has offices dedicated to AI diplomacy, but the current administration has taken a sharply oppositional stance toward multilateral AI governance, declining to sign the Paris AI summit declaration. AI diplomacy is secondary to the broader foreign policy mission.

Established by the National AI Initiative Act of 2020 with a statutory mandate to coordinate all federal AI activities, but significantly sidelined under the current administration as Sacks and Kratsios drive AI policy directly from the White House. Its statutory mission remains AI-focused but its actual influence is limited.

GAO has produced nearly 50 AI products since 2018 including the AI Accountability Framework (2021) and 20+ ongoing AI projects, but it cannot compel agency compliance or create binding rules—its power is through authoritative oversight reporting and recommendations. AI oversight is a top institutional priority.

scored.player: ODNI (Office of the Director of National Intelligence)
scored.rationale: ODNI coordinates AI strategy across all 18 intelligence elements, appointed its first Chief AI Officer (John Beieler) in 2024, and established the AIM group for governance and oversight. It has meaningful domain-specific influence over intelligence community AI adoption but limited reach beyond national security.

FDA has authorized nearly 1,000 AI-enabled medical devices and issued major draft guidance in January 2025 on AI for drug development. It wields significant regulatory authority within health AI but has no jurisdiction over AI governance broadly. AI regulation is a growing institutional priority.

The bipartisan House and Senate AI Caucuses serve primarily educational and convening roles—they lack legislative authority and cannot mark up bills. However, they have been consistently dedicated to AI policy education, with 15 bills becoming law under the Senate caucus since 2019.

scored.player: Sen. Ted Cruz (R-TX) — Chair, Commerce Committee
scored.rationale: As Chair of the Senate Commerce Committee—the committee with primary AI jurisdiction—Cruz wields major gatekeeping power over AI legislation. He released a five-pillar "Legislative Framework for American Leadership in AI" (September 2025) and introduced the SANDBOX Act. His interest is high and sustained.

scored.player: Sen. Maria Cantwell (D-WA) — Ranking Member, Commerce
scored.rationale: As Ranking Member, Cantwell retains significant influence through procedural tools and coalition-building. She led the successful 99-1 vote to strip the AI moratorium from the reconciliation bill in July 2025 and pushed seven bipartisan AI bills through committee in the prior Congress. AI is clearly a top legislative priority.

Schumer was the architect of the Senate's AI legislative push, convening nine AI Insight Forums and organizing the Bipartisan AI Working Group. As Minority Leader since January 2025, his procedural power is reduced, but he retains significant floor influence and can shape Democratic priorities on AI.

Blumenthal co-led the first bipartisan AI legislative framework (with Hawley) in 2023, held landmark hearings with Sam Altman and Dario Amodei, and in 2025 co-introduced the AI Risk Evaluation Act. He has meaningful influence through advocacy and media presence but limited procedural power as a minority member.

Hawley chairs the Judiciary Subcommittee on Privacy, Technology, and the Law, co-led bipartisan AI legislative efforts with Blumenthal, and introduced the GUARD Act to ban AI chatbot companions for minors. His populist approach to Big Tech gives him distinctive influence within the GOP.

scored.player: Sens. Todd Young (R-IN), Mike Rounds (R-SD), Martin Heinrich (D-NM)
scored.rationale: These three senators co-authored the May 2024 AI Policy Roadmap and are the Senate's most dedicated AI policy voices. Rounds co-chairs the Senate AI Caucus and introduced a five-bill AI package in 2025. Collectively influential in agenda-setting, though individually they lack major committee chairmanships.

Obernolte chairs the House Science Committee's Research and Technology Subcommittee (jurisdiction over NIST/CAISI), co-chaired the bipartisan House AI Task Force producing 89 recommendations, and is developing the "Great American AI Act" to codify CAISI. AI is clearly his primary legislative focus.

Lieu is one of Congress's most vocal AI advocates and one of few members with a computer science background. He co-chaired the bipartisan House AI Task Force and now co-chairs the House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy. As a minority member, his legislative power is limited.

Beyer co-chairs the Congressional AI Caucus, served on the bipartisan House AI Task Force, and co-introduced the CREATE AI Act with Obernolte. He works through bipartisan partnerships rather than committee authority, and AI is a sustained top priority in his legislative portfolio.

SASC controls defense AI policy through the must-pass annual NDAA. The FY2026 NDAA contains extensive AI provisions including an AI Futures Steering Committee and model assessment frameworks, passed 26-1. However, the committee's AI focus is narrowly defense-oriented and AI is one of many defense priorities.

scored.player: House Science, Space, and Technology Committee
scored.rationale: This committee has jurisdiction over NIST (including CAISI), NSF, and OSTP. In 2024, it marked up nine AI bills, and Subcommittee Chair Obernolte is advancing legislation to codify CAISI in January 2026. Its power is meaningful but limited to authorization of science/standards agencies.

scored.player: House Energy & Commerce Committee (Chair: Brett Guthrie)
scored.rationale: Energy & Commerce has the broadest House jurisdiction, covering FTC, FCC, consumer protection, health AI, and telecom. Under Guthrie, the committee has held multiple AI hearings and articulated three pillars: "dominance, deployment, and safeguards." AI competes with energy, health, and other priorities.

scored.player: House Foreign Affairs Committee (Chair: Brian Mast)
scored.rationale: The committee advanced the AI OVERWATCH Act in January 2026 with bipartisan support (42-2-1), creating congressional oversight of advanced AI chip exports. It has genuine influence over export controls and international AI competition, but jurisdiction is narrower than Commerce or E&C.

Led by Schumer, Rounds, Heinrich, and Young, this group convened nine AI Insight Forums and produced the May 2024 AI Policy Roadmap. Its influence has been primarily agenda-setting, and its organizational status in the 119th Congress is unclear as Schumer moved to Minority Leader. Congress has not passed comprehensive AI legislation despite the roadmap.

Home to 32 of the world's 50 leading AI companies, California enacted 24 AI-related laws in 2024–2025 including SB 53 after SB 1047's veto. Newsom convened expert advisory groups and Wiener continues driving frontier model legislation, giving California de facto national regulatory reach.

Colorado passed the first comprehensive U.S. AI consumer protection law (signed May 2024, delayed to June 2026), establishing a model EU AI Act-style risk-tiered framework that other states reference. Its limited economic leverage constrains direct power, but the state remains very actively engaged through AG rulemaking and legislative refinement.

NY signed the RAISE Act on December 19, 2025, becoming the second state with frontier AI safety legislation. The law creates an oversight office, mandates 72-hour incident reporting, and imposes $1M–$3M penalties. Combined with California, this creates a de facto national standard.

Governor Abbott signed TRAIGA on June 22, 2025 (effective Jan. 1, 2026), making Texas the fourth state with a cross-sectoral AI law. The final version was dramatically pared back from the original EU AI Act-style proposal to an innovation-friendly, intent-based liability framework, reflecting meaningful but moderate engagement.

Utah is a pioneering first-mover, creating the nation's first permanent Office of AI Policy, the AI Learning Laboratory (first state regulatory sandbox), and the first state AI disclosure law. This model is being watched nationally but Utah's smaller economic footprint limits its direct market-forcing power.

Illinois has enacted several targeted AI laws including HB 3773 (AI employment discrimination, effective Jan 2026) and the 2019 AI Video Interview Act, and has a Generative AI Task Force. As a large state it carries weight, but its approach is sector-specific rather than comprehensive.

Virginia passed HB 2094 (comprehensive high-risk AI bill), but Gov. Youngkin vetoed it in March 2025 citing innovation concerns. However, Youngkin issued Executive Order 51 launching the nation's first agentic AI regulatory reduction pilot, showing meaningful but ideologically divided engagement—more innovation-focused than safety-focused.

Connecticut was an early mover with SB 1103 (signed June 2023, establishing an Office of AI), and Sen. Maroney's comprehensive SB 2 passed the Senate but stalled after Gov. Lamont's veto threat. Maroney has been a key architect of multi-state AI legislative coordination, showing substantial dedicated activity despite limited governance power.

Tennessee enacted the ELVIS Act (March 2024), the first-of-its-kind law adding "voice" to right-of-publicity protections against AI cloning, passed unanimously. While precedent-setting in its narrow domain, it represents a single domain-specific intervention rather than broad AI governance engagement.

Oregon enacted HB 2299 criminalizing AI-generated intimate images and SB 1571 requiring election deepfake disclosure. These are important but narrowly targeted actions that provide some influence through state precedent without representing a comprehensive AI governance agenda.

This bipartisan coalition has been the single most effective force blocking federal preemption of state AI laws—successfully removing the AI moratorium from both the Big Beautiful Bill and the NDAA in 2025. They retain broad enforcement authority under existing consumer protection statutes and have demonstrated willingness to litigate.

NCSL tracked 1,208 AI-related bills across all 50 states in 2025 (145 enacted) and facilitates interstate coordination and information exchange. While it has no direct policymaking authority, it shapes state legislative agendas through authoritative tracking and convening functions.

Cox has made AI governance a signature issue—signing pioneering Utah AI legislation, launching the "Pro-Human AI" initiative, investing $10M in AI workforce readiness, and vocally opposing federal preemption at national forums. As a Republican governor championing state AI regulation, he provides unique bipartisan credibility.

Brown co-leads the national Bipartisan AG AI Task Force alongside NC AG Jeff Jackson, partnering directly with OpenAI and Microsoft on AI accountability frameworks. His direct industry engagement and national convening role give him meaningful influence, though power is exercised through coalition and voluntary collaboration.

scored.player: Zach Boyd — Director, Utah Office of AI Policy
scored.rationale: Boyd directs the nation's first permanent state AI policy office and administers the AI Learning Laboratory (first state regulatory sandbox), executing regulatory mitigation agreements with companies and building an institutional model other states are watching. AI governance is his sole professional mission, but direct power is limited to one state's administrative authority.

Implements the world's first comprehensive binding AI regulation (EU AI Act) with enforcement powers including fines up to 7% of global turnover, coordinates governance across 27 member states, and has 140+ staff across five units including a dedicated AI Safety unit. AI governance is its primary mission.

China's multi-agency regulatory apparatus has enacted multiple binding AI regulations since 2021 covering algorithms, deep synthesis, and generative AI, with active enforcement campaigns and mandatory filing requirements. It proposed a Global AI Cooperation Organization in 2025. AI governance is a major state priority intertwined with economic growth and geopolitical competition.

scored.player: UK AI Security Institute (Dir: Adam Beaumont)
scored.rationale: AISI has tested 30+ frontier models, holds partnerships with Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google DeepMind, and leads international safety institute coordination—but operates in an advisory/evaluative capacity (£66M/year) without legislative or enforcement authority. AI safety is its exclusive and primary mission.

Hosted the Paris AI Action Summit (Feb 2025) with 100+ countries and launched INESIA (National AI Safety Institute) in January 2025. As a major EU member state, France significantly shapes EU AI Act implementation, and Macron drove the €200B InvestAI initiative. AI governance is a substantial priority balanced against aggressive innovation promotion.

Passed the world's second comprehensive AI law (Dec 2024, effective Jan 2026) establishing a National AI Committee under the president, an AI Safety Institute, and risk-based obligations for high-impact AI. Implementation details and subordinate legislation are still being developed. AI governance is a top legislative priority with strong bipartisan support.

The third major economy to enact AI legislation, Japan launched the Hiroshima AI Process during its G7 presidency and established an AI Strategy Headquarters under the PM. However, the AI Promotion Act imposes no penalties and relies on voluntary guidelines, reflecting significant diplomatic influence but deliberately light-touch domestic regulation.

India is the world's 2nd-largest LLM user, co-chaired the Paris AI Action Summit, and launched the IndiaAI Mission ($1.2B+) with AI Governance Guidelines released Nov 2025. However, the AISI is still being established, guidelines are non-binding, and no dedicated AI legislation exists. Engagement is growing substantially but still in early implementation.

Singapore pioneered the world's first government-developed AI testing toolkit (AI Verify), published Model AI Governance Frameworks for traditional AI, GenAI, and Agentic AI, and leads the ASEAN Working Group on AI Governance. As a small city-state with voluntary frameworks and no AI-specific laws, influence is primarily normative rather than regulatory.

CAISI launched Nov 2024 with $50M over five years, leveraging world-class researchers including Yoshua Bengio and Geoffrey Hinton. Canada is a founding member of the International Network of AISIs, but AIDA legislation remains pending and CAISI is still building operational capacity. Strong research influence but limited enforcement capability.

Released the National AI Plan in Dec 2025 with three pillars and announced an AI Safety Institute with ~A$30M funding. However, Australia has no AI-specific legislation, relies on existing laws with a "light touch" approach, and the AISI is still being established. Engagement is growing but early-stage.

Germany has invested €5B in AI through 2025 and as the EU's largest economy, its implementation of the EU AI Act will significantly shape enforcement practices. However, its AI governance operates primarily through the EU framework rather than independent national regulation, with focus more on research competitiveness than safety-specific governance.

The Senate approved comprehensive AI Bill No. 2338/2023 in Dec 2024 (risk-based, modeled on EU AI Act), now under review in the Chamber of Deputies. Brazil's $4B AI investment plan and regional influence give it growing clout as Latin America's largest economy, but the law remains unenacted and enforcement infrastructure is embryonic.

Israel ranks #1 globally in AI talent concentration and established a National AI Directorate under the PMO in Oct 2025. However, it has no dedicated AI law, no binding regulations, and governance institutions are nascent. The approach remains deliberately soft-law and innovation-first with limited global regulatory influence.

scored.player: UAE (Ministry of AI; Mohamed bin Zayed AI Initiative)
scored.rationale: The UAE appointed the world's first Minister of State for AI (2017), launched AI Strategy 2031, and created the world's first AI-enabled Regulatory Intelligence Office (April 2025). It has little AI-specific legislation and limited global enforcement footprint, but exceptional institutional investment and ambition.

The OECD AI Principles (2019, updated 2024) are the first intergovernmental standard on AI, endorsed by 47 jurisdictions. The Observatory tracks over 1,000 AI initiatives across ~70 jurisdictions and now hosts the merged GPAI partnership. Its definitions underpin the EU AI Act, G7 Hiroshima Process, and other frameworks. Standards are voluntary but normatively powerful.

The 39-expert body released "Governing AI for Humanity" (September 2024) proposing seven recommendations including an International Scientific Panel on AI. The Global Digital Compact adopted by 193 member states established the Scientific Panel as a direct outcome. However, it is a temporary advisory mechanism with no binding authority or enforcement power. AI governance was its sole mission.

UNESCO produced the first global standard on AI ethics (Recommendation on the Ethics of AI, 2021), unanimously adopted by all 193 member states—the broadest country adoption of any AI governance instrument. Implementation tools exist but the Recommendation is non-binding and enforcement mechanisms are limited. AI ethics is significant but not sole priority within UNESCO's broad mandate.

SC 42 is the world's premier international AI standards committee with 60+ participating countries and 55+ published standards covering AI management systems (ISO/IEC 42001), trustworthiness, governance, and risk management. Standards are referenced in the EU AI Act and become de facto requirements through regulatory adoption. The committee's entire mandate is AI standardization.

WEF launched the AI Governance Alliance (350+ members, 280+ organizations) in June 2023 and publishes influential frameworks including the Presidio AI Framework. It shapes global discourse and provides platforms for public-private collaboration, but has no regulatory or binding authority. AI governance is a major initiative but WEF covers hundreds of global issues.

Produced the world's first international framework for advanced AI governance within 7 months, including Guiding Principles and a Code of Conduct for advanced AI developers. The G7's work has directly shaped domestic regulation in the US, Japan, and EU. The OECD now monitors the Code through a formal Reporting Framework. Limitation is lack of permanent institutional structure between rotating presidencies.

Originally launched in 2020, GPAI merged with the OECD's AI work in July 2024 to form an integrated partnership of 44 countries across six continents. The merger gives it OECD institutional backing, a stable secretariat, and 500+ member expert community. Outcomes remain non-binding and consensus-driven, but broad legitimacy.

scored.player: AI Safety Summit Process (Bletchley > Seoul > Paris > India)
scored.rationale: Achieved landmark outcomes: Bletchley Declaration (28 countries including US and China), creation of AI Safety Institutes, Frontier AI Safety Commitments from 16 companies (Seoul), and the International Scientific Report on AI Safety. The only AI governance forum involving both the US and China from inception. Paris 2025 saw tensions as scope broadened away from pure safety; India hosts the next summit (Feb 2026).

scored.player: International Network of AI Safety Institutes
scored.rationale: Launched at the Seoul Summit (May 2024) with 10 members (US, UK, EU, Japan, Canada, France, South Korea, Singapore, Australia, Kenya). Focuses on synthetic content risks, foundation model testing, and risk assessments. Still nascent—most member AISIs are only months old with modest budgets—but AI safety is its sole and exclusive mission.

Adopted a revised AI Strategy (July 2024) with six Principles of Responsible Use and an Autonomy Implementation Plan covering 32 allied nations, giving it significant influence over military AI norms; however, AI safety governance is secondary to collective defense, and NATO is unlikely to pursue arms control for military AI.

As the UN's specialized agency for digital technologies (194 member states), ITU hosts the AI for Good Summit, publishes the Annual AI Governance Report, and co-leads the AI Standards Exchange Database with ISO/IEC/IEEE; however, its focus spans broad AI governance rather than specifically safety-critical domains like CBRN or frontier model safety, and its standards are voluntary.

Developed the most comprehensive technical AI safety standards through the IEEE 7000 series (including P7009 on fail-safe design), launched Global Initiative 2.0 embedding a "Safety First Principle," created the CertifAIEd certification program, and established an "AI Safety Champions" community; while these standards are voluntary and lack regulatory force, safety-critical AI governance is an explicit primary focus increasingly integrated into regulatory processes like the EU AI Act.

Operates in 170+ countries conducting AI Landscape Assessments and runs an AI Trust and Safety Re-imagination Programme, but influence on safety-critical AI governance is indirect through capacity building; its AI work centers on equitable development and digital inclusion rather than frontier model safety or existential risk.

Published authoritative guidance including "Ethics and Governance of AI for Health" and "Guidance on Large Multi-Modal Models" (2024/2025), and actively calls for legal safeguards on AI-as-medical-device; within its health domain, safety is explicitly central, though influence is limited to the healthcare sector.

Co-developed a Policy Framework for Responsible Limits on Facial Recognition with the WEF and published a "Toolkit for Responsible AI Innovation in Law Enforcement"; however, it has no regulatory authority, and its AI governance engagement is narrowly focused on biometrics and law enforcement tools rather than broader safety-critical concerns.

UN-mandated multi-stakeholder dialogue platform (10,000+ annual participants) with a dedicated Policy Network on AI producing annual governance briefs; however, IGF produces no binding outcomes, standards, or regulations—it is purely deliberative with minimal direct governance influence.

Sets industry norms through its updated Preparedness Framework V2 (April 2025), massively scaled lobbying ($1.76M in 2024, ~$1.7M in H1 2025 alone—a 7x increase from 2023), and Altman's Congressional testimony shapes the national AI policy narrative; however, the framework's CEO override provisions, removal of persuasion as a tracked risk, and a lobbying shift toward energy/infrastructure/deregulation indicate safety governance is substantial but secondary to commercial ambitions.

Founded explicitly on AI safety and pioneered the Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP) framework with AI Safety Levels that influenced industry-wide norms; RSP V3.0 includes Frontier Safety Roadmaps and external review commitments, though in February 2026 it dropped its flagship pledge to halt training if safety measures were inadequate, citing competitive and Pentagon contract pressure—weakening but not eliminating its leading safety-governance posture.

Operates within Alphabet ($7.8M lobbying in H1 2025) with its Frontier Safety Framework V3 covering misuse, ML R&D, and misalignment risks; co-founded the Frontier Model Forum, signed Seoul AI Safety Commitments, and maintains both an AGI Safety Council and Responsibility and Safety Council—substantial and institutionalized safety engagement operating alongside Google's broader commercial priorities.

scored.player: Meta AI (CEO: Mark Zuckerberg; Chief Scientist: Yann LeCun)
scored.rationale: Top tech lobbyist ($13.8M in H1 2025) whose open-weight Llama models fundamentally reshape the governance landscape; published a Frontier AI Framework (Feb 2025) and provides safety tools, but Chief Scientist LeCun is AI's most prominent skeptic of catastrophic risk, actively opposes regulation of open-source AI, and Meta's governance interest is primarily defensive—preventing regulation that constrains its open-source strategy rather than proactive safety governance.

As the world's most valuable tech company and OpenAI's primary backer (~$13B+ invested), Microsoft has a Chief Responsible AI Officer, published annual Responsible AI Transparency Reports, introduced a Frontier Governance Framework (2024), and maintains ISO/IEC 42001 certification; AI safety governance is one meaningful priority among many across its massive enterprise, cloud, and defense portfolio, focused more on responsible deployment and compliance than frontier catastrophic-risk mitigation.

As the dominant AI chip maker (first company to reach $4T market cap), Nvidia exercises structural power since its GPUs are essential for training frontier models and US export controls on its chips are themselves a major governance mechanism; however, Huang opposes AI-specific governance, advocates sector-specific regulation by existing agencies, and the company has no published frontier AI safety framework comparable to peers.

Musk's political proximity to Trump and massive compute resources give xAI significant influence; the company signed an MOU with the US AI Safety Institute and employs CAIS director Dan Hendrycks as safety advisor, but actions contradict safety rhetoric—a Feb 2026 talent exodus, deepfake controversies, and antisemitic output incidents reveal a persistent gap between advocacy and practice.

Dominant cloud provider hosting frontier models via Bedrock and investing $50B in government AI infrastructure; endorsed White House Voluntary AI Commitments, signed the EU AI Act Code of Practice, and joined the Frontier Model Forum, but governance engagement is commercially motivated and framed as compliance tooling rather than a safety-first mission.

scored.player: Palantir (CEO: Alex Karp; co-founder: Peter Thiel)
scored.rationale: Structurally embedded in US defense AI with a $10B Army Enterprise Agreement, $1B+ Maven contracts, and NATO deals, amplified by Thiel's political influence; however, Palantir has no published AI safety framework, no participation in voluntary safety commitments, and treats governance as a customer-defined responsibility.

R1 release shook global markets and demonstrated frontier-class open-source capabilities; CEO Liang Wenfeng met with Xi Jinping and DeepSeek signed China's AI Safety Commitments (Dec 2024), but evaluations remain unpublicized, it was absent from the second round of CnAISDA commitments, and lacks a published safety framework comparable to Western peers.

scored.player: Scale AI (Founder: Alexandr Wang, now at Meta)
scored.rationale: Holds $340M+ in government contracts including DOD Thunderforge and a $100M CDAO agreement for AI T&E up to TS/SCI environments, giving it a unique role in military AI evaluation; however, Wang departed as CEO in June 2025 when Meta acquired a 49% stake for $14.3B, leaving the company in leadership transition with diminished near-term policy influence.

Europe's leading AI startup ($14B valuation) with outsized EU governance influence—CEO sat on France's Commission on AI, lobbyist Cédric O shaped EU AI Act open-source exemptions, and Mistral signed the GPAI Code of Practice; however, it also led a 45-company letter asking the EU to delay AI Act enforcement, revealing tension between compliance engagement and regulatory resistance.

Controls 1.5B+ active devices and App Store gatekeeping, with a Nov 2025 guideline update regulating third-party AI data sharing across all iOS apps; built a responsible AI framework with 12 risk categories and deploys on-device/Private Cloud Compute models, but is relatively disengaged from public AI safety policy advocacy and international governance forums, preferring to lead through product design.

Made AI governance a core business strategy with watsonx.governance named a Leader by Forrester and IDC MarketScape; participated in the EU High-Level Expert Group on Trustworthy AI, helped develop the NIST AI RMF, exited facial recognition on ethical grounds, and co-founded the AI Alliance; however, its diminished market position relative to hyperscalers limits direct governance power.

Controls critical AI compute infrastructure (MI300/MI400 GPUs) and Lisa Su testified before the Senate Commerce Committee on "Winning the AI Race" (May 2025); however, policy engagement centers on competitiveness, export controls, and the CHIPS Act rather than AI safety governance specifically.

Spends $2M+/quarter on federal lobbying with 64 lobbyists, has deep government ties (Larry Ellison close to Trump administration, FedRAMP High authorized), and lobbies on AI innovation policy and export controls; however, AI safety governance is a small subset of its broad portfolio spanning healthcare IT, cybersecurity, tax, and trade.

Signed the EU GPAI Code of Practice, received $240M from Canada's Sovereign AI Compute Strategy, and its Chief AI Officer was appointed to Canada's AI strategy task force; however, its $7B valuation gives it modest power compared to hyperscalers, and CEO Gomez has stated existential AI risk warnings are "overblown."

Remains active under CEO Prem Akkaraju and is listed as a GPAI model provider under the EU AI Act; however, after leadership upheaval, co-founder fraud lawsuits, and a commercial pivot to film/gaming enterprise deals, it has minimal proactive AI safety governance engagement and limited influence.

Has become a central cautionary case driving AI safety legislation—lawsuits alleging teen suicides, Congressional hearings, an FTC complaint, and California's chatbot safety law were all catalyzed by its platform's harms; it implemented safety measures reactively (separate under-18 LLM, suicide prevention pop-ups) but engagement is driven by legal pressure rather than proactive mission.

Effectively hollowed out in March 2024 when Microsoft hired co-founders Suleyman and Simonyan plus most staff and paid $650M for a technology license—a "pseudo-acquisition" that triggered FTC and CMA investigations; the remaining entity has minimal independent governance influence, having been reduced from a $4B-valued lab to an enterprise licensing shell.

Hosts 1.5M+ public AI models serving 7M+ users with a dedicated policy team (including Head of Global Policy Irene Solaiman); submitted detailed White House AI Action Plan response advocating open-source AI for safety, launched RiskRubric.ai for standardized model risk assessment, and argues "safety cannot scale if it remains proprietary"—making AI safety governance a core organizational priority.

Convenes all six leading frontier AI developers with a singular mission of advancing frontier AI safety; allocated $10M+ through the AI Safety Fund, pioneered CBRN risk evaluation frameworks, published comprehensive technical reports on frontier safety, and established a first-of-its-kind information-sharing agreement for AI vulnerabilities—directly shaping industry safety norms that governments reference.

Deployed 91 AI lobbyists in 2025—more than any other entity—and leads the push for federal preemption of state AI laws with its influential "Hidden Cost of 50 State AI Laws" report; however, its focus is on business-friendly, innovation-promoting governance rather than safety-critical AI specifically, treating AI as one priority among many.

A 50+-member coalition with a Trust and Safety working group (230+ participants) that produced an AI evaluation taxonomy and safety guide; however, its core mission is democratizing open AI innovation rather than safety-critical governance, and it lacks direct regulatory or lobbying influence.

scored.player: BSA / The Software Alliance (CEO: Victoria Espinel)
scored.rationale: Represents major AI companies (Microsoft, OpenAI, Salesforce, Oracle) across 20+ markets with $2.7M+ in annual lobbying; actively shapes governance through detailed policy filings on the EU AI Act, Trump AI Action Plan, and state legislation, but AI safety is one priority alongside cybersecurity, IP, and data privacy.

A 140+-member multi-stakeholder organization with a dedicated Safety Critical AI program producing influential frameworks including "Guidance for Safe Foundation Model Deployment"; shapes norms referenced by policymakers but operates as a best-practices body without direct lobbying power, and has faced criticism as potentially industry-captured.

scored.player: ITI (Information Technology Industry Council; CEO: Jason Oxman)
scored.rationale: Global tech trade association with offices in Washington, Brussels, Delhi, and Seoul directly shaping AI governance—most notably active on the EU AI Act's GPAI Code of Practice compliance; represents leading AI companies with $2.7M+ lobbying, but AI safety is one area among a broad tech policy portfolio.

The most effective state-level tech advocacy organization, engaging on 808 bills across all 50 states in 2025 with an 87% success rate; most vocally opposes state AI safety regulation and champions federal preemption, though interest centers on preventing regulatory fragmentation rather than advancing safety-critical governance specifically.

Represents Google, Amazon, Meta, and Intel with 50+ years of advocacy for innovation-friendly policy; actively engages on state AI legislation and EU AI Act implementation but AI governance is one of many policy areas alongside competition, copyright, privacy, and trade.

With a $125M+ war chest funded by OpenAI's Greg Brockman and a16z, this PAC directly targets candidates who sponsor state AI safety bills—its first target being NY Assemblymember Alex Bores (RAISE Act sponsor); represents an unprecedented use of electoral spending to shape AI safety governance, though still unproven in actual election cycles.

Laser-focused on AI security with ~50 staff, $55M+ Open Philanthropy founding grant, direct Congressional testimony by Toner (House Judiciary Subcommittee, 2025), and prolific publications including "AI Governance at the Frontier" (Nov 2025); widely regarded as a trusted nonpartisan source for policymakers on US-China AI competition and frontier evaluation, though as a university-based center it influences policy indirectly.

Wields significant influence through its globally cited AI Index Report (8th edition, 2025), Congressional AI boot camps for senior staffers, and deep Silicon Valley connections; Marietje Schaake as International Policy Director adds global governance reach, but AI safety governance is one focus among many (education, healthcare, economics, climate).

Gained significant credibility by hiring Elham Tabassi (former NIST Chief AI Advisor, lead author of the AI Risk Management Framework) as AIET director in March 2025; among DC's most influential think tanks with strong convening power, but AI is one initiative among dozens, and AIET's broad scope dilutes specific focus on safety-critical governance.

scored.player: CNAS (Center for a New American Security)
scored.rationale: Leading voice on military AI and autonomous weapons governance through its AI and Global Security Initiative; Paul Scharre (author of "Army of None") is widely influential, and its AI Task Force co-chaired by former Deputy SecDef Robert O. Work brings high-level credibility, though focus is primarily defense/national security rather than broad civilian AI safety.

Produced directly relevant publications including "Governance Approaches to Securing Frontier AI" (Oct 2025, examining 4 compliance models) and "Strategies and Detection Gaps in a Game-Theoretic Model of Compute Governance"; as a major FFRDC with direct government contracts, its analysis feeds into decision-making, but AI governance is one area among an extremely broad portfolio.

Has significant political influence on the Trump administration (Project 2025 mirrored in ~two-thirds of early executive actions) and its Center for Technology and the Human Person engages on AI policy; however, AI safety governance engagement is occasional, framed through a pro-innovation and anti-overregulation lens rather than deep technical safety analysis.

scored.player: Center for AI Safety (CAIS) (Dir: Dan Hendrycks)
scored.rationale: Primary mission is reducing societal-scale AI risks; its 2023 "Statement on AI Risk" was cited as a catalyst for the UK AI Safety Summit, and its Action Fund co-sponsored California's SB 1047 and launched a DC presence engaging bipartisan lawmakers; however, as a nonprofit it lacks direct regulatory or binding power, influencing through agenda-setting, technical research (WMDP benchmark, circuit breakers), and compute infrastructure for 100+ safety researchers.

scored.player: METR (Model Evaluation & Threat Research; founder: Beth Barnes)
scored.rationale: Entire mission is assessing catastrophic risks from frontier AI; conducts pre-deployment evaluations for OpenAI and Anthropic whose results appear in official system cards, prototyped the Responsible Scaling Policy framework now adopted by 9 leading developers, and provides technical assistance to NIST AISI, UK AISI, and European AI Office; its "time horizon" metric has become central to AI forecasting, giving it outsized technical authority that directly informs deployment decisions.

Prestigious foreign policy think tank whose Technology and International Affairs Program produces influential research on international AI governance architectures, US-China competition, and comparative regulation; received Open Philanthropy funding for AI governance research, but AI governance is a significant though not dominant focus alongside nuclear proliferation, climate, democracy, and many other issues.

Newly launched Technology Policy Accelerator (directed by Amy Zegart, backed by Condoleezza Rice) has strong influence in conservative/Republican policy circles with direct access to policymakers and Silicon Valley; however, its AI engagement emphasizes innovation and US competitiveness, with safety-critical governance at most a peripheral concern within a broader technology-and-national-security portfolio.

Prominent center-right national security think tank whose AI work focuses on US-China competition, export controls on AI chips, and AI as a geopolitical/military asset rather than safety governance per se; hosts events on strategic AI imperatives but has limited engagement with safety-specific frameworks like alignment, evaluations, or responsible scaling.

Convenes elite AI governance roundtables through Aspen Digital and launched the Technology Leaders Initiative (2025) with Oxford's Institute for Ethics in AI; however, AI safety governance is one topic among many, and its influence is primarily through convening rather than direct policy-making or advocacy.

scored.player: ITIF (Information Technology and Innovation Foundation; Rob Atkinson)
scored.rationale: Ranked the world's top science and technology think tank by UPenn; highly prolific in DC AI policy debates advocating federal preemption and opposing EU-style regulation, with frequent Congressional testimony; its counter-regulatory influence is significant in shaping what safety governance does and does not pass.

scored.player: GovAI (Centre for the Governance of AI, Oxford; Dir: Ben Garfinkel)
scored.rationale: One of the first academic groups to study AI governance, with influential research on safety frameworks, compute governance, and AGI governance; alumni populate key positions at Google DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic, CSET, and CSIS, creating meaningful indirect influence on governance outcomes through talent pipelines, though it lacks the direct policy access of DC-based institutions.

Leading independent research institute tracking AI compute trends, model capabilities, and scaling forecasts with 100+ outputs in 2025; data directly used by policymakers and frontier labs, with EU AI Act compute thresholds informed by their work; their FrontierMath benchmark and Capabilities Index are critical infrastructure for safety governance, but influence is indirect—providing empirical foundations rather than advocating specific policies.

Dedicated AI safety advocacy organization producing highly cited polling (73% of voters want AI company liability, 80% favor mandatory safety testing), played a visible role in the SB 1047 debate, and effectively channels public concern into legislative pressure; as a newer and smaller organization, it influences through media narratives and public opinion data rather than direct institutional power.

One of the most prestigious US foreign policy institutions with a dedicated Digital and Cyberspace Policy Program, senior AI fellows (including a former NSA chief AI officer), and the publisher of Foreign Affairs; its 2026 AI analysis collection examines safety, sovereignty, and military AI governance, giving it significant influence on senior policymakers, but AI safety remains one of many foreign policy priorities.

scored.player: CSIS (Center for Strategic and International Studies)
scored.rationale: Named the #1 international security think tank for nine consecutive years, CSIS operates the dedicated Wadhwani AI Center (since 2023) led by Gregory C. Allen, producing high-impact research on AI national security, autonomous weapons, and compute governance; provides Congressional testimony and private briefings, and is a trusted resource for DC policymakers on AI safety and security.

Congressionally chartered center running AI Lab and Advanced AI Labs that have trained 1,200+ Congressional and Executive Branch staffers since 2016 on AI topics including safety and security; bridges technologists and policymakers through its Science and Technology Innovation Program, giving it meaningful capacity-building influence, though it does not directly advocate for specific policies.

Operates in formal partnership with the US State Department (AI Connect program promoting OECD AI Principles), launched a GeoTech Commission on AI with bipartisan Congressional co-chairs and top industry executives; has meaningful convening power and government partnerships but exercises indirect rather than binding influence, with AI governance as a core but not exclusive focus.

Produces research on AI accountability and filed substantive regulatory comments (e.g., NTIA dual-use AI models urging open-source support); however, AI governance is one of several priorities alongside digital equity, broadband access, and privacy, limiting concentrated influence in the AI safety governance space.

Highly prolific on AI regulation with Senior Fellow Adam Thierer testifying before House Judiciary and Energy & Commerce Committees in 2025; advocates for a "learning period moratorium" on AI regulation and federal preemption, significantly shaping center-right AI governance discourse, though influence is advisory rather than binding.

Takes the most libertarian position, arguing government should be "cut out of AI governance to the greatest extent possible" and criticizing even Trump-era approaches as insufficiently deregulatory; produces less dedicated AI governance output than peer think tanks and exercises ideological rather than direct policy influence.

Published a comprehensive AI agenda ("An AI Agenda for a New Era," July 2025) offering a centrist Democratic alternative to Trump's deregulation, with a dedicated AI policy advisor; aims to influence centrist Democratic lawmakers but AI is a relatively recent addition to its broad portfolio spanning climate, economy, and social policy.

Has a dedicated AI Technology project, trained 600+ Congressional staff through AI 101 (2024), submitted White House AI Action Plan recommendations, and launched an AI and Energy Task Force with Microsoft, Google, and Nvidia; bipartisan convening power and direct Congressional education role give it meaningful but non-binding influence.

scored.player: AI Now Institute (NYU; ED: Amba Kak)
scored.rationale: AI governance is AI Now's sole mission; co-EDs served as Senior Advisors on AI to FTC Chair Lina Khan, Kak addressed the UN General Assembly on AI governance (Sept 2025), and leadership regularly advises Congress, the White House, and EU/UK governments; uniquely independent (refuses all corporate funding) with significant agenda-setting power, though lacking binding authority.

scored.player: CDT (Center for Democracy & Technology; CEO: Alexandra Reeve Givens)
scored.rationale: Operates a dedicated AI Governance Lab (led by Miranda Bogen) directly engaging policymakers, companies, and multi-stakeholder initiatives on AI auditing, safety evaluation, and transparency, with offices in Washington DC and Brussels actively shaping the EU AI Act; primary orientation is civil rights and democratic values rather than catastrophic safety risks specifically, though work on high-risk AI systems creates meaningful overlap.

Wields significant influence through its massive membership, litigation capacity, and political clout—actively challenging Trump's AI Action Plan and supporting the AI Civil Rights Act of 2025; however, AI engagement is predominantly framed through civil rights and anti-discrimination (facial recognition bans, algorithmic bias), with limited direct focus on catastrophic AI safety risks.

Engages in AI policy through litigation, amicus briefs, and legislative advocacy—notably filing AI Action Plan comments and defending Anthropic's refusal to allow unrestricted military AI use (Feb 2026), its most direct safety-governance engagement; however, AI work is primarily oriented toward digital rights, privacy, and free expression rather than safety-critical governance specifically.

scored.player: Future of Life Institute (FLI; co-founder: Max Tegmark)
scored.rationale: Entire mission centers on reducing catastrophic and existential AI risk; publishes the AI Safety Index (3rd edition, Winter 2025, evaluating 8 companies across 35 indicators), organized the famous 2023 pause letter, issued the October 2025 Superintelligence Statement signed by five Nobel laureates, and funds PhD fellowships in AI Existential Safety; elevated agenda-setting power for a nonprofit, though cannot unilaterally create binding governance outcomes.

scored.player: Center for Humane Technology (Tristan Harris, Aza Raskin)
scored.rationale: Expanded significantly from social media harms to AI safety risks including manipulation, existential risk, and loss of human agency; "AI Dilemma" presentation briefed to heads of institutions, podcast highly influential, and 2026 "AI and What Makes Us Human" launched as a new focus; directly engages catastrophic AI risk narratives and participates in the WEF AI Governance Alliance, though influence is derived primarily from narrative-shaping rather than institutional access.

scored.player: EPIC (Electronic Privacy Information Center) / CAIDP (Marc Rotenberg)
scored.rationale: EPIC is a veteran privacy/digital rights organization using litigation, FOIA actions, and FTC engagement to challenge facial recognition and algorithmic systems; CAIDP publishes the comprehensive 1,500-page AI and Democratic Values Index covering 80 countries with 1,000+ contributors, referenced by UNESCO and the Council of Europe; combined, they have substantial influence through both domestic and international channels, with primary lens on democratic values and privacy rather than catastrophic AI safety per se.

Landmark Gender Shades research directly caused IBM to halt facial recognition sales, and Buolamwini testified before Congress and advised Biden before his AI Executive Order; has had outsized cultural and policy impact through research and media, but focus is principally on algorithmic bias and equity rather than safety-critical AI governance in the catastrophic/existential risk sense.

Gebru's thought leadership and the landmark "Stochastic Parrots" paper give DAIR outsized cultural influence, but as a small independent nonprofit it lacks direct regulatory or legislative power. AI ethics and countering Big Tech's influence on AI R&D is their entire mission.

Deploys significant resources ($30M+ in AI investments, Mozilla.ai, policy advocacy across US/EU), with substantial brand recognition and active policymaker engagement at the Paris AI Summit, EU Digital Summit, and White House RFIs. Trustworthy AI is a top organizational priority alongside broader internet health and open web issues.

Despite being a youth-led volunteer organization (~900 members across 40+ countries), Encode Justice helped draft the White House Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights, secured AI provisions in the 2025 NDAA, worked with NIST, and published the "AI 2030" vision endorsed by Bengio. AI safety governance is the organization's singular mission.

Significant convening power through RightsCon (3,500–4,000 attendees), active participation in UN-level governance processes, advocacy for binding human rights-based AI frameworks, and support for outright bans on facial recognition and biometric surveillance. AI and human rights governance is a major programmatic focus alongside broader digital rights work.

scored.player: Center for AI and Digital Policy (CAIDP; Marc Rotenberg)
scored.rationale: Outsized influence through the annual "AI and Democratic Values" Index covering 80 countries (1,500+ pages), the FTC complaint that triggered the OpenAI investigation, and advisory roles with OECD, Council of Europe, European Parliament, and UNESCO. AI governance and democratic values is literally CAIDP's entire raison d'être.

Produces influential empirical research through programs like "AI on the Ground" and "Labor Futures," and publishes policy briefs analyzed by governance actors, but primarily operates as a research institute without direct policy-making authority. AI governance is central to their research agenda, particularly automated systems' institutional impacts.

Data-driven investigations have directly influenced governance—their mortgage discrimination investigation was cited by CFPB, DOJ, and OCC, and by senators introducing the Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2022—but as a journalism outlet it exercises indirect influence through exposing harms rather than direct advocacy. Algorithmic accountability is the newsroom's core editorial mission.

Announced in 2025 that it is winding down operations, redirecting focus to partner coalition Design It For Us. Before closing, co-released the influential "Zero Trust AI Governance" framework with AI Now Institute and EPIC. AI governance was one of several priorities within their broader Big Tech accountability mission; given the wind-down, current engagement is minimal.

As the largest US online racial justice organization (7M+ members), wields significant political pressure through mass mobilization, corporate campaigns, and its comprehensive "Black Tech Agenda" (2022, updated 2024) including detailed AI policy demands endorsed by members of Congress. AI/technology accountability is a growing priority but competes with criminal justice reform, voting rights, and other core racial justice priorities.

Uniquely combines CS, data science, law, and public policy expertise in Washington, DC; partners with civil rights organizations and policymakers, has received multiple MacArthur Foundation grants for AI policy work, and convened a December 2024 federal AI policy meeting. Advancing equity and justice in AI governance is Upturn's core mission.

Pioneered bias bounty and red teaming methodologies adopted by governments and industry (partners include Google.org, NVIDIA, Meta, UNESCO); Chowdhury's standing as U.S. Science Envoy for AI and testifier at Schumer's AI Insight Forum amplifies the organization's reach. AI accountability and responsible evaluation is their core and essentially only mission.

The only organization globally focused solely on supporting frontier AI whistleblowers, operating a "Third Opinion" anonymous expert service and "Publish Your Policies" campaign that pressured Anthropic and others; contributed to the 2025 FLI AI Safety Index and EU AI Act whistleblowing provisions. Very small and niche, but fills a unique role. AI safety governance via whistleblower protections is 100% of their mission.

Youth-led coalition with meaningful visibility through congressional testimony, CA AADC passage, and KOSA advocacy; published an AI Policy Framework in 2024. Primary focus remains social media platform accountability for youth, with AI as a growing but secondary concern alongside broader tech reform.

Small but highly active organization that co-sponsored landmark state AI safety legislation—California's SB 53 (signed into law) and New York's RAISE Act (passed legislature)—and works directly with lawmakers to develop bill text. Influence primarily at state level and through coalition participation. AI safety legislation is their singular organizational mission.

Brings enormous institutional credibility, a massive membership base, and deep regulatory relationships; Brookman has testified before multiple Senate committees on AI fraud and deepfakes, and CR has published comprehensive AI policy recommendations and investigated algorithmic pricing. AI governance is one priority among many (product safety, privacy, data security, financial services).

Deep grassroots networks, significant political leverage, and legal mobilization capacity give the NAACP substantial weight when it engages on AI; published an AI healthcare equity governance framework, issued policy briefs on predictive policing, and passed a comprehensive AI resolution. However, AI governance is an emerging focus alongside a vast portfolio of civil rights priorities.

scored.player: Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law
scored.rationale: Established as a backbone organization for civil rights–focused AI governance through its dedicated Digital Justice Initiative; co-authored and co-leads the AI Civil Rights Act coalition (with Sen. Markey and Reps. Clarke, Pressley, Jayapal), drafted the Online Civil Rights Act model legislation, and co-led a major coalition response to Trump's EO on AI preemption. AI governance via civil rights lens is a top organizational priority with sustained dedicated staffing.

Small nonprofit with outsized influence: Nkonde was lead advocate for the Algorithmic Accountability Act (2019), participated in Schumer's AI Insight Panels, attended White House AI roundtables, and pioneered shareholder activism on AI at Microsoft and Apple. Small organizational size limits direct institutional power. AI governance through a racial justice lens is their core mission.

scored.player: MIRI (Machine Intelligence Research Institute; founder: Eliezer Yudkowsky)
scored.rationale: Significant agenda-setting and cultural influence—Yudkowsky is the most recognized figure in AI existential risk discourse, their 2025 book became a NYT bestseller, and their Technical Governance Team engages with EU AI Act Code of Practice, Canadian Parliament, and UN on AI verification. However, their maximalist "halt all AI development" position and nonprofit constraints on lobbying limit direct governance power. Preventing human extinction from AI is their entire raison d'être.

scored.player: Alignment Research Center (ARC; founder: Paul Christiano)
scored.rationale: Small, highly theoretical research organization focused on formal mechanistic explanations of neural network behavior; its indirect influence is significant: **Christiano became Head of AI Safety at NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation**, and ARC incubated ARC Evals (now METR), which conducts frontier model evaluations for companies and governments. AI alignment is their exclusive mission.

Small nonprofit producing highly cited AI safety research (AI control, alignment faking detection) that directly shapes practices at Anthropic and DeepMind, and partnered with UK AISI on safety cases. Lacks regulatory authority, giving it indirect influence through technical contributions. AI safety is their sole organizational mission.

Meaningful direct influence through partnerships with OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Microsoft, Amazon, UK AISI, and the US AISI Consortium for scheming evaluations; research presented at the UK AI Safety Summit and cited by the SEC Chairman and Yoshua Bengio before the US Senate. AI safety governance—especially deceptive alignment evaluation—is Apollo's entire mission.

Small for-profit startup (~$10M VC funded) with limited research output compared to peers, but CEO Connor Leahy is a prominent public advocate who addressed the UK House of Lords, co-founded ControlAI advocacy group, and pushed for compute caps and AI kill switches. AI safety is their core mission, combining technical work with active governance advocacy.

UK charity operating in the niche but increasingly important domain of multi-agent AI safety; their 2025 Technical Report co-authored with 40+ leading researchers is establishing multi-agent risks as a governance concern. Influence remains domain-limited and grant-making scale is modest. Multi-agent cooperation and safety is their primary mission.

Punches above its weight by incubating the Safe AI Forum (IDAIS), facilitating critical China-West AI safety dialogues, hosting policy-connecting conferences, and producing widely cited robustness research (adversarial Go attacks, LLM jailbreaking). Remains a small nonprofit with limited direct regulatory influence. AI safety is FAR.AI's sole mission across its full portfolio.

Very small (8-person, ~$2.5M budget), early-stage nonprofit founded October 2023, focused on highly theoretical research applying singular learning theory to AI alignment. Minimal current governance engagement, policy influence, or connection to regulatory processes. Work is currently far upstream from direct governance applications. AI safety alignment is their exclusive mission.

Small Berkeley-based nonprofit (~5 staff) whose influence significantly exceeds its size through producing the largest expert surveys of AI researchers (2,778 participants in 2023), widely cited in governance discussions; founder Katja Grace named among Time's 100 most influential in AI. Functions as an information provider rather than direct governance actor.

Prestigious Oxford research department with significant influence through the Governance of Emerging Technologies programme (Prof. Wachter's fairness metric influenced the EU AI Act), written evidence to UK Parliament, and the Oxford Commission on AI & Good Governance. AI governance is one of several major research streams alongside digital politics, misinformation, and platform regulation.

scored.player: Future of Humanity Institute (FHI) — Oxford (Closed April 2024)
scored.rationale: Historically a Power 7–8 actor that essentially created the fields of existential risk, AI alignment, and AI governance, but closed in April 2024 after Oxford imposed hiring/fundraising freezes. Legacy persists through alumni (GovAI spun out 2021, Toby Ord continues at Oxford Martin) and foundational concepts, but as a closed institution it can no longer directly influence governance. AI safety governance was FHI's raison d'être for 19 years.

scored.player: Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence — Cambridge
scored.rationale: Well-resourced interdisciplinary center (£10M Leverhulme Trust grant) with multi-university collaborations (Oxford, Imperial, UC Berkeley), an MPhil in Ethics of AI, and links to CSER and policymakers. Operates primarily in academic rather than direct policy channels. Research spans algorithmic transparency, autonomous weapons regulation, value alignment, and AI ethics.

scored.player: MIT AI Policy / MIT AI Risk Initiative
scored.rationale: Created the comprehensive AI Risk Repository (1,700+ risks from 74 frameworks) and the AI Governance Map analyzing 950+ governance documents, providing widely used reference tools for policymakers, regulators, and auditors globally. Highly influential as a research resource and taxonomic standard-setter, but lacks direct regulatory or legislative authority.

Launched the Ethics and Governance of AI Initiative (jointly with MIT Media Lab), produced the Principled AI mapping project, and supports inclusive AI governance frameworks globally—regularly cited by policymakers. AI governance is one of several major research areas alongside internet governance, free speech, and disinformation.

Directed by Helen Nissenbaum, explores AI ethics and governance, privacy, and unfair discrimination through postdoctoral fellowships and interdisciplinary research; researchers have submitted comments to NTIA and NIST on AI accountability policy. A smaller, focused initiative whose policy influence is real but indirect and primarily channeled through academic publications.

scored.player: George Washington University — Institute for AI, Technology & Law
scored.rationale: Houses the Trustworthy AI initiative (GW TAI) and is a core partner in the NSF-funded TRAILS Institute (with UMD, Morgan State, Cornell) that collaborates with NIST on AI benchmarks. DC proximity provides access, but its direct policy influence is still developing. Note: AI governance work is dispersed across GW TAI, TRAILS, and the Center for Law and Technology.

Houses the prominent Privacy Engineering Program offering degrees and certificates in privacy engineering and AI governance, teaches courses covering EU AI Act, NIST RMF, and ISO frameworks, and connects to the Block Center for Technology and Society's Responsible AI focus. Influence comes primarily through producing technical governance practitioners, tools, and research.

Led by Julia Stoyanovich, focuses on making responsible AI "synonymous with AI" through research on fairness, transparency, and accountability; Stoyanovich testified at the US Senate's AI Insight Forum and contributed to NYC's algorithmic accountability efforts. As a single university center, direct ability to influence major governance outcomes is limited to advocacy, tools, and norm-building.

Co-directed by Robert Trager and Michael Osborne, with affiliates holding senior roles at the EU AI Office, UK AI Safety Institute, and international standards bodies. Helped set the agenda for the Global AI Summit series, submitted detailed policy comments on the US AI Action Plan, and launched a Technical AI Governance DPhil programme. AI governance is its exclusive, primary mission.

Independent research institute (Nuffield Foundation) that has been a dominant voice in UK AI governance, producing foundational reports ("Regulate to Innovate," "Regulating AI in the UK"), submitting extensive written evidence to Parliament, and conducting nationally representative polling on public attitudes toward AI regulation. One of the most influential non-governmental voices on UK/European AI governance.

The UK's national institute for data science and AI, co-authored the UK Government's official Public Sector Guidance on AI Ethics and Safety, operates the AI Standards Hub, advises the ICO on AI fairness, and runs the AI Ethics and Governance in Practice Programme. National institute status gives it meaningfully higher power than most academic institutions, though AI ethics/governance is one area alongside broader data science and AI research.

scored.player: UC Berkeley CHAI (Center for Human-Compatible AI)
scored.rationale: Founded by Stuart Russell (author of the standard AI textbook), singularly focused on ensuring AI systems are provably beneficial; Russell serves as vice chair of WEF Council on AI, member of GPAI, co-chair of OECD Expert Group on AI Futures, and advisor to UN agencies. Power derives primarily from thought leadership and Russell's personal stature rather than institutional regulatory authority.

World-class deep learning research (140+ affiliated professors), partnerships with UNESCO and the UN, contributions to the International AI Safety Report, and a dedicated AI Governance, Policy and Inclusion program including an AI Policy Fellowship. Lacks direct regulatory authority. AI safety and governance is a declared strategic priority with a VP of Policy, Safety and Global Affairs.

scored.player: One-U Responsible AI Initiative — University of Utah (Manish Parashar)
scored.rationale: A $100M university initiative led by Utah's inaugural Chief AI Officer, funding interdisciplinary RAI research, fellowships, and the external RAI Community Consortium. Influence on AI governance is primarily through research, workforce development, and state-level coalitions. Responsible AI is the initiative's core mission.

Independent, volunteer-led cooperative launched during Utah Tech Week 2025 uniting academia, government, industry, and civil society through Special Interest Groups on policy, workforce, and infrastructure; released an AI Leadership Blueprint and responded to a federal RFI. State-level, volunteer-driven with no regulatory authority and nascent national influence. Responsible AI governance is its sole raison d'être.

scored.player: a16z / Andreessen Horowitz (Marc Andreessen, Ben Horowitz)
scored.rationale: **Per Bloomberg (Feb 2026), a16z has "near-veto power over virtually all AI-related legislative proposals."** Federal lobbying surged to $3.53M in 2025 (double 2024), the firm manages $90B in assets, helped draft Trump's executive order on state AI preemption, launched a $100M+ super PAC, and has former partners in key government positions (David Sacks as AI czar, Sriram Krishnan as senior White House AI advisor). AI governance is treated as existential to its business model with dedicated policy staff.

Enormous financial leverage as chairman of the **$500B Stargate Project** (with OpenAI, Oracle, MGX), ~$41B invested in OpenAI (11% stake), and majority owner of Arm Holdings. Son stood alongside Trump at the White House to announce Stargate. Direct engagement with AI safety governance specifically is limited; focus is primarily on AI investment, infrastructure, and commercial deployment rather than safety regulation.

scored.player: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy; Dustin Moskovitz & Cari Tuna)
scored.rationale: **Largest philanthropic funder of AI safety research worldwide**, with 480+ grants through its Navigating Transformative AI fund and over $4B in total grants. Active RFPs for technical AI safety research (~$40M per round), funded foundational organizations including CAIS, ARC, GovAI, and CSET ($100M+). Published October 2025 white paper "AI Safety and Security Need More Funders." Shapes the field through funding rather than direct regulatory authority.

Led the largest-ever VC defense investment ($1B into Anduril at $30.5B valuation) with immense political access: Thiel co-founded Palantir, his protégé JD Vance is Vice President, his former aide Michael Kratsios is OSTP Director. "Reports little to no federal lobbying, yet its network holds outsized sway in the Pentagon and White House" (TechCrunch). Direct engagement with AI *safety* governance is limited—influence runs through defense/national security channels and deregulatory political networks.

scored.player: Gulf Cooperation Council Sovereign Wealth Funds (PIF, MGX/Mubadala, ADIA, QIA)
scored.rationale: Collectively deployed **$66B in AI/digital investments** by 2025, ~63% of global sovereign AI spending; PIF allocated $40B for AI, MGX has $100B earmarked, and MGX is a Stargate Project partner. Massive capital deployment gives significant structural influence over what AI gets built and where. Engagement with AI safety governance specifically is minimal—focus is economic diversification, infrastructure, and geopolitical positioning.

Former Google CEO, #1 ranked family office in AI investing (22+ firms), chaired the US National Security Commission on AI whose 700+ page report shaped Biden-era policy, and founded the Special Competitive Studies Project as a successor. Schmidt Futures funds AI safety through AI2050 initiative (grants up to $5M for AI risk research). High-level access across administrations, though critics flag conflicts of interest between investments and policy influence.

Following OpenAI's October 2025 restructuring, holds a **26% equity stake ($130B)** in OpenAI Group PBC with exclusive governance control—appoints all board members, controls the Safety and Security Committee which can veto model releases, and can override commercial decisions. One of the most structurally powerful entities in AI safety governance, controlling safety practices at the world's leading frontier AI company. Real-world independence from commercial pressure remains untested.

Umbrella for EA-aligned AI safety giving including the Long-Term Future Fund and AI Risk Mitigation Fund, channeling millions into safety research and advocacy. Significant influence over the AI safety research ecosystem through funding, but lacks regulatory authority or direct corporate leverage, and suffered reputational damage from the FTX collapse. AI safety is the literal primary mission of these funds.

scored.player: Reid Hoffman — LinkedIn co-founder, Microsoft board
scored.rationale: Co-founder of LinkedIn, co-founder of Inflection AI, early OpenAI board member; regularly engages policymakers (CFR, LSE lectures, White House) and authored "Superagency" (2025) advocating iterative deployment and measurement-based regulation. Lacks institutional regulatory power and represents one voice among many tech leaders. Substantial AI governance activity alongside broader investment and entrepreneurial activities.

Early investments in OpenAI and Anthropic give meaningful influence through capital allocation; publicly clashes with lawmakers (called Sanders/Khanna "morons" over AI regulation) and shapes industry narrative. Explicitly dismisses existential AI risk and favors light-touch regulation, making his engagement with safety-critical governance moderate and counter-directional to most safety advocacy.

Commands enormous public influence as Microsoft co-founder; Gates Foundation committed $1.4B to farming AI and $5M AI/LLM initiative for LMICs. Views on AI governance (opposes development pauses, warns about bioterrorism risks from open-source AI) receive massive global attention. AI safety governance is one concern alongside primary focuses of global health, climate, and education.

Launched August 2025 with **$500M over five years from 10 major US foundations** (MacArthur, Ford, Mellon, Mozilla, Omidyar, Packard). One of the largest coordinated philanthropic AI responses, but brand new—hasn't begun pooled-fund grantmaking (starting 2026)—so actual power to materially influence outcomes remains prospective. Explicitly and solely dedicated to ensuring people-centered AI governance.

America's largest labor federation (63 unions, ~15M workers) with significant political lobbying power; launched the October 2025 "Workers First Initiative on AI" (the first comprehensive labor AI agenda) and the AFL-CIO Technology Institute. Actively lobbied against federal AI preemption provisions and mobilizes members on state AI legislation. AI has become a top organizational priority with dedicated institutional infrastructure.

Won **landmark AI protections through the 2023 strike** (informed consent and compensation for digital replicas, notification for synthetic performers), expanded protections through subsequent agreements, secured California legislation (AB 2602 and AB 1836), and lobbies for the federal NO FAKES Act. Domain-leading force in creative/entertainment AI governance, though influence is sector-limited.

Their 146-day 2023 strike resulted in the **first-ever union contractual AI protections** (AI cannot be credited as writer, AI-generated material cannot be source material, studios must disclose AI use), becoming a widely cited model for labor AI negotiations across industries. Both WGA West and East actively pursue policy advocacy through Copyright Office filings, Senate letters, and endorsed the TRAIN Act.

Represents ~2M workers; meaningful advocacy through its framing of "algorithmic guidance" over "algorithmic management" and active participation in state-level AI workplace legislation (SEIU Local 668 worked with PA Governor Shapiro on groundbreaking AI agreement in March 2025). AI is one priority among the union's wider labor agenda, but engagement is substantial and growing.

~1.3M members; meaningful influence through 2023 UPS contract provisions (technology review committee, restrictions on surveillance, automation protections), active lobbying on autonomous vehicle legislation in multiple states, and opposition to federal AI preemption. AI/automation concerns are increasingly prominent but focused primarily on autonomous vehicles and workplace surveillance rather than broader safety governance.

Among the first US unions to publish formal AI Principles for the Workplace (Dec 2023) and secured a first-of-its-kind AI agreement with Microsoft/ZeniMax, demonstrating meaningful but sector-limited influence through collective bargaining. AI governance is now a substantial organizational priority with a dedicated AI Committee, published principles, and active integration into contract negotiations across telecom, tech, and journalism.

1.8M members; launched the **$23M National Academy for AI Instruction** (July 2025) with Microsoft, OpenAI, and Anthropic—the first national union-tech company partnership of its kind—targeting 400,000 educators. AI governance in education has become a top priority with formal AI resolution and direct partnerships with AI companies, though influence is limited to the education sector.

3M members make it the largest professional employee organization in the US with significant political advocacy power; approved a formal AI policy statement (July 2024) with five key principles, created an AI Task Force, and advocates for federal/state legislation on AI transparency. Few teacher contracts yet address AI, limiting practical implementation power. AI remains one priority among many.

Represents 176M+ workers in 161+ countries and engages with the ILO, G7/G20, and OECD on labor standards, giving it a significant global advocacy platform. Published October 2025 report calling for full union involvement in AI deployment globally. No direct regulatory authority; operates through declarations, reports, and coalition pressure. AI governance is one emerging issue among many broader labor rights priorities.

scored.player: Yoshua Bengio — Mila / Universite de Montreal
scored.rationale: **Chairs the International AI Safety Report** (2025 and 2026 editions, backed by 30+ countries and 100+ experts), giving him unparalleled agenda-setting influence on global scientific consensus around AI risks. Founded LawZero (June 2025), testified before the US Senate, serves on the UN Scientific Advisory Board, and contributed to the Montreal Declaration for Responsible AI. AI safety governance is now his primary professional mission.
