Nobel Prize (2024) and Turing Award credentials make him the most prominent individual voice warning about AI existential risks; departed Google in 2023 to speak freely, co-signed the Shanghai Consensus on AGI risk management, and estimates ~20% chance of AI causing human extinction. Advocates for government-mandated safety research, international cooperation treaties, and compute threshold licensing. Holds no institutional position with direct governance authority.

scored.player: Yann LeCun — Meta AI, Chief Scientist
scored.rationale: Turing Award winner and former Meta chief AI scientist (left late 2025 to launch Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs); exerts significant influence on governance debates by actively opposing restrictive safety regulation and championing open-source AI. Co-signed the Mozilla open AI letter, testified at the UN, criticized the UK AI Safety Summit's existential risk focus, and opposed California's SB 1047. Engagement is substantial but **counter-directional to most AI safety governance efforts**—dismissing many safety concerns as "complete BS."

Co-director of Stanford HAI, member of the UN Scientific Advisory Board, **TIME Person of the Year 2025**, and Queen Elizabeth Prize for Engineering recipient. Stanford HAI produces highly influential policy whitepapers, runs Congressional AI Boot Camps, and Li has testified multiple times before the US Senate and addressed the UN Security Council. Advocates for science-based AI policymaking and public sector research funding.

As CEO of the world's most prominent AI company (**700M+ weekly active users, $20B+ annualized revenue, $110B funding round**), Altman can unilaterally shape governance outcomes through company decisions; has direct access to world leaders, testified before the US Senate, and OpenAI's policy positions substantially influence the legislative agenda. Regularly engages with governance, but critics document tension between OpenAI's commercial acceleration and safety commitments; warned government approval for model releases would be "disastrous."

CEO of Anthropic ($61.5B valuation, $7B revenue); developed the **Responsible Scaling Policy** (first major company to do so), publicly confronted the Pentagon over red lines on autonomous weapons and mass surveillance, and published influential essays ("Machines of Loving Grace," "Adolescence of Technology"). Left OpenAI specifically over safety concerns, pioneered Constitutional AI and RSPs as governance prototypes, and publicly called for stronger regulation while criticizing proposed moratoriums. AI safety governance is Anthropic's founding mission and Amodei's personal driving purpose.

World's richest person with direct Trump administration access via DOGE, xAI's $200M Pentagon contract, and lawsuits against OpenAI give near-unilateral governance influence; however, his engagement is primarily competitive and self-serving rather than systematically safety-focused, contradicted by racing to build frontier AI.

Leads Google DeepMind (backed by Alphabet's ~$2T market cap) and won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry; developed the Frontier Safety Framework, committed to pre-release model access for the UK AISI, and advocates for international AI safety cooperation at Bletchley Park and Paris summits, though constrained by Google's commercial priorities.

Co-founded IASEAI (inaugurated Feb 2025 with 700+ attendees), co-chairs OECD and WEF AI groups, sits on the UN Secretary-General's AI Advisory Body, contributed to the International AI Safety Report, and was named TIME100 AI (2023, 2025); AI safety governance is unambiguously his primary mission, though he lacks direct regulatory or market leverage.

Influence operates through landmark research ("Gender Shades," "Stochastic Parrots"), the TESCREAL critique framework, and DAIR Institute's work on AI harms to marginalized communities; however, DAIR is a small foundation-funded nonprofit without regulatory, market, or legislative leverage, limiting direct governance power.

Gender Shades research compelled Amazon, IBM, and Microsoft to halt facial recognition sales to law enforcement — rare direct policy impact; continues governance work through AJL's 2025 TSA facial recognition study, congressional testimony, and NAACP LDF Board election (2025), though limited by AJL's small organizational scale.

scored.player: Meredith Whittaker — President, Signal; AI Now co-founder
scored.rationale: As Signal president (used by governments, militaries, journalists globally), AI Now co-founder, and former Senior Advisor on AI to FTC Chair Lina Khan, Whittaker holds meaningful institutional leverage; actively engages at the Paris AI Action Summit (2025), Davos, and UN forums, warning agentic AI poses an "existential threat" to privacy.

scored.player: Kate Crawford — USC / AI Now co-founder
scored.rationale: Foundational intellectual voice through "Atlas of AI" (translated into 12 languages), advisory roles with the UN, White House, European Parliament, and Spanish PM's AI Council; named TIME100 AI (2023) and awarded Venice Biennale Silver Lion (2025), but influence is through agenda-setting and intellectual framing rather than direct regulatory authority.

Gained prominence through May 2023 Senate testimony alongside Sam Altman, widely-read Substack "Marcus on AI," and founding CATAI with Omidyar Network backing; however, he lacks regulatory authority or market power, and Congress has not passed legislation he advocates for. AI governance has become his primary public mission since 2023.

scored.player: Helen Toner — Interim ED, Georgetown CSET
scored.rationale: Leads a 50-person policy center ($55M Open Philanthropy funding), testified before multiple Congressional committees, gained worldwide prominence through the 2023 OpenAI board crisis and TIME 100 AI (2024); AI safety governance is her primary professional mission, though influence remains advisory rather than decisional.

FLI president with a well-funded organization ($665M Buterin donation), organized the landmark 2023 Pause Letter, released the FLI AI Safety Index 2024 grading major labs, and advocates for an FDA-like AI oversight body; AI existential risk governance is his singular focus, though FLI remains an advocacy organization without direct governance authority.

scored.player: Marietje Schaake — Stanford HAI, former EU Parliament member
scored.rationale: Serves on the UN High-Level Advisory Body on AI (Executive Committee), is International Policy Director at Stanford's Cyber Policy Center, former 10-year MEP, FT columnist, author of "The Tech Coup," and special advisor to EU VP Vestager — giving meaningful transatlantic governance influence in advisory rather than decision-making capacities.

scored.player: Rumman Chowdhury — Humane Intelligence; Harvard Berkman Klein Center
scored.rationale: As CEO of Humane Intelligence, US Science Envoy for AI (Biden admin), Harvard Berkman Klein Fellow, and former Twitter ML Ethics Director, Chowdhury has meaningful influence through government advisory roles and algorithmic auditing practice; responsible AI governance is her primary professional mission, pioneering community-driven AI auditing and operationalizing NIST risk frameworks.

Author of influential "Algorithms of Oppression" (2018) and MacArthur Fellow directing UCLA's Center on Race & Digital Justice, Noble shapes academic discourse on algorithmic bias; however, her engagement is primarily with algorithmic discrimination and racial equity — important but adjacent to safety-critical AI governance of frontier models, and she does not regularly participate in AI safety summits or standards bodies.

Globally recognized AI leader (8M+ students, Amazon board member since 2024) who testified before the Senate AI Insight Forum and successfully lobbied against California's SB 1047 (vetoed by Newsom); his platform gives outsized weight in governance debates, but he engages primarily as a skeptic of heavy regulation, and governance is one of many priorities alongside education and entrepreneurship.

Director of Princeton's CITP, co-author of the acclaimed "AI Snake Oil" book (2024, Nature top 10), and TIME 100 AI honoree; his frameworks for distinguishing genuine AI capabilities from hype are referenced by FTC staff and informed EU AI Act discussions, but his work is broader than safety-critical frontier AI governance specifically.

Holds one of the most consequential positions in AI safety governance as Head of Safety at NIST's US AI Safety Institute, directly shaping federal AI safety standards; also founded the Alignment Research Center, served on the UK Frontier AI Taskforce advisory board, and is principal architect of RLHF. Every professional activity centers on ensuring advanced AI systems are safe and aligned.

"Superintelligence" (2014) was foundational in catalyzing the AI safety field, but FHI closed in April 2024, personal controversies diminished his reputation, and his new Macrostrategy Research Initiative is small and early-stage; he remains interested in existential AI risk but is less actively engaged in current governance processes compared to his peak influence.

Intellectual progenitor of the AI alignment field; MIRI pivoted to policy/communications in 2023–2024, published a governance research agenda in 2025, and his co-authored NYT bestseller "If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies" (2025) reached mainstream audiences — but extreme positions (global moratorium, GPU restrictions) limit mainstream policy traction. AI existential risk is his singular life's work.

scored.player: Dan Hendrycks — Center for AI Safety
scored.rationale: Orchestrated the landmark "Statement on AI Risk" (2023), develops widely-used benchmarks (MMLU, Humanity's Last Exam), advises xAI and Scale AI, co-authored "Superintelligence Strategy" with Eric Schmidt, and published the 2024 textbook "Introduction to AI Safety, Ethics, and Society"; AI safety governance is his singular career mission, though he lacks direct regulatory authority.

Commands enormous public influence as a globally bestselling author (Sapiens, Homo Deus, Nexus) with Davos and UN speaking platforms, but influence is discursive — shaping public opinion rather than directly crafting policy. His 2024 book "Nexus" made AI risks to democracy his central focus, though AI governance remains one of several civilizational themes he addresses.

As co-founder and Head of Policy at Anthropic, Clark has direct influence over how a leading frontier lab approaches safety; briefed the UN Security Council on AI threats (2023), testified before Congress multiple times, served on the US National AI Advisory Committee, and co-chairs Stanford's AI Index — rare dual influence as industry insider and policy architect.

Testified before Congress multiple times (2023–2025), served as Senior AI Advisor at the FTC, spoke before the UN General Assembly on AI governance (2025), attended the Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit, and named TIME 100 AI 2024; AI governance is her primary mission at AI Now, focusing on corporate concentration and regulatory capture, though she operates from a civil society position.

scored.player: Daron Acemoglu — MIT (2024 Nobel in Economics)
scored.rationale: The 2024 Nobel Prize gives uniquely authoritative platform on AI's economic implications; "Power and Progress" (2023) and papers on AI productivity shape high-level discourse, but he operates as an academic economist rather than a direct governance participant, with AI alongside his broader research on institutions and inequality.

Foundational existential risk thinker whose "The Precipice" and research helped spawn the Centre for the Governance of AI; estimates >10% chance of unrecoverable AGI catastrophe this century and advocates for international coalitions and government emergency powers. FHI's April 2024 closure reduced his institutional base, and he now operates primarily as an academic researcher.

scored.player: Tristan Harris — Center for Humane Technology
scored.rationale: Massive public reach (The Social Dilemma reached 100M+ viewers in 190 countries), regularly briefs heads of state and Congress, and CHT supports strategic litigation on AI chatbot harms; since 2023, Harris has substantially pivoted CHT from social media harms to AI risks, with a 2026 initiative on AI and fundamental rights.

Wields significant dual influence — its landmark copyright lawsuit against OpenAI (filed Dec 2023, proceeding to trial 2025) is directly shaping AI copyright law and data governance precedent, while its extensive AI beat (Roose, Metz, Hard Fork podcast) sets the public narrative on AI governance for policymakers and the public alike.

Bloomberg Law's AI regulation trackers, EU AI Act compliance guides, and state-by-state legislation snapshots are widely used by lawyers and policymakers as authoritative reference resources; Bloomberg's financial coverage also shapes how investors and markets respond to AI regulation.

Platformer newsletter (200,000+ weekly readers including tech executives and government officials) and co-hosting Hard Fork give meaningful influence among tech-literate policymakers; recent coverage includes deep pieces on authoritarian AI, Pentagon AI contracts, and international governance summits, showing sustained AI safety governance engagement.

Broad influence in tech policy circles through podcasts, CNN contributor role, major conferences, and direct access to tech CEOs and policymakers; advocates for tech regulation and a Department of Technology but is a generalist commentator rather than a governance specialist, engaging AI regulation alongside many other tech topics.

His viral 2023 Bing chatbot conversation became a landmark moment in public AI safety awareness; his NYT column and Hard Fork podcast reach millions, and his forthcoming book on the AGI race (2026) demonstrates sustained deep engagement with AI-specific governance and safety discourse.

NYT's dedicated AI industry reporter with deep source network across Google, Meta, OpenAI, and Anthropic, and author of "Genius Makers"; his coverage of DeepSeek, AI data practices, and Meta's internal governance debates is influential, though his focus skews more toward industry dynamics than regulatory policy specifically.

Tiku's investigative reporting consistently reveals industry practices that become governance flashpoints — investigating xAI's loosened safety guardrails, Anthropic's data practices, and how tech PACs shape AI regulation; WaPo's institutional reach among DC policymakers amplifies impact on governance discourse.

Will Knight is Wired's senior AI writer (previously MIT Technology Review) with deep expertise spanning ML advances, China's AI landscape, and tech policy; invited to speak at ITU's AI for Good Global Summit, signaling recognition in governance circles, though Wired's influence on actual policymaking is more limited than NYT or Bloomberg.

As an individual newsletter writer, Zvi lacks direct governance authority, but his extraordinarily detailed weekly AI analysis (tracking legislation, company commitments, PAC spending) shapes elite discourse among policymakers, journalists, and researchers; AI safety governance is effectively his full-time primary mission with dedicated governance spinoff posts and deep legislative analysis.

Highly respected technology publication with dedicated AI policy reporters (Melissa Heikkilä, Zeyi Yang) producing influential analyses of global AI regulation (EU AI Act, US executive orders, China's rules); MIT's institutional brand lends significant credibility, though AI governance is part of broader technology journalism coverage.

Key information source for US and EU policymakers with POLITICO Pro generating ~$8M in US federal government subscriptions and dedicated tech policy teams in Washington and Brussels; hosts annual AI & Tech Summits, but AI governance is one of many policy beats alongside healthcare, energy, and defense.

Dedicated AI regulation tracking tools, "In Focus: Artificial Intelligence" portal, and expert legal analysis reports covering state AI laws, EU AI Act compliance, and AI hallucination litigation are widely used by legal professionals and corporate counsel shaping compliance strategy; AI tracking sits alongside Bloomberg Law's vast broader legal coverage.

scored.player: AI Snake Oil (newsletter) — Arvind Narayanan & Sayash Kapoor
scored.rationale: With **60,000+ readers** including researchers, policymakers, and journalists, a bestselling Princeton University Press book named to Nature's top 10, and TIME 100 AI recognition, the newsletter wields notable thought-leadership influence on how policymakers understand AI capabilities versus hype; directly informs safety-critical policy discourse on predictive AI governance and algorithmic discrimination.

A 70,000-reader weekly newsletter with unique insider knowledge given Clark's role as Anthropic co-founder and Head of Policy; covers measurement frameworks, compute governance, and policy interventions in detail. Scored here as a media product — its influence is enhanced by but distinct from Clark's personal governance roles (scored separately as Actor 21).

Leading forum for rigorous legal analysis of AI governance at the national security intersection, with a dedicated "Scaling Laws" AI podcast and senior editors publishing detailed analyses of military AI rules, the DPA-Anthropic dispute, and AI regulation frameworks; widely read by legal scholars, national security professionals, and congressional staff.

The 16+ consolidated lawsuits (MDL consolidation of 12 cases in SDNY ordered April 2025) are establishing binding legal precedents on fair use, data disclosure, and AI training — the Thomson Reuters v. Ross Intelligence ruling and NYT v. OpenAI data preservation order are already reshaping governance through judicial precedent, though most cases remain unresolved.

Launched its Center for Digital Health and AI in October 2025, released a comprehensive 8-step AI governance toolkit, and actively lobbied Congress as physician AI usage doubled from **38% to 70%** between 2023–2024; influence is domain-limited to healthcare and relies on professional guidance rather than binding rules.

Has approved over **1,250 AI-enabled medical devices** (as of July 2025), published comprehensive draft guidance on AI lifecycle management (January 2025), finalized Predetermined Change Control Plan guidance (December 2024), and created a Digital Health Advisory Committee; wields real binding regulatory authority, though domain-limited to medical products.

Munich Re's dedicated Insure AI team (since 2018) and aiSure™ product suite create de facto governance through underwriting requirements — described by Munich Re's own experts as "soft regulation" signaling model quality through premium pricing; market-based influence is growing, though they remain private-sector actors without regulatory enforcement power.

The Big Four are investing billions (KPMG $2B, PwC $1.5B, EY $1.4B) to position as premier AI auditors — KPMG Australia became the first organization worldwide to receive ISO/IEC 42001 certification — and will serve as critical intermediaries for EU AI Act compliance, though AI assurance is one of many service lines.

scored.player: Legal associations (ABA AI task force, state bar ethics)
scored.rationale: The ABA created its AI Task Force in August 2023, issued landmark Formal Opinion 512 on generative AI ethics (July 2024), and dozens of state bars have issued AI ethics guidance; influence is professional-normative — shaping how lawyers use and govern AI through ethics rules and competence standards — rather than direct policymaking.

scored.player: Faith communities (Vatican 'Rome Call for AI Ethics')
scored.rationale: The Rome Call has expanded dramatically — signed by Microsoft, IBM, Cisco, extended to 11 world religions at Hiroshima (July 2024), with Fr. Paolo Benanti appointed to the UN Advisory Body on AI; wields significant moral authority and global audience reach but lacks regulatory or economic levers, operating through norm-setting and moral suasion.

Institutional investors managing trillions are increasingly evaluating AI as a material ESG risk, with WEF's Responsible AI Playbook and CFA Institute guidance; however, US political headwinds have dampened momentum — the SEC withdrew proposed AI and ESG rules under the Trump administration — and AI governance remains one consideration among many ESG factors.

NYC's Local Law 144 (enforced July 2023) was the first US law requiring bias audits for AI hiring tools, and the LOADinG Act (2024) requires oversight of government AI systems — critical testing grounds for governance innovation; however, enforcement has proven weak (a December 2025 audit found "null compliance"), limiting practical impact.

ISO/IEC 42001 (December 2023) became the first international certifiable AI management system standard, with ISO/IEC 42005 adding AI impact assessments (April 2025); these bodies provide the technical backbone translating the EU AI Act and other regulations into implementable, auditable requirements — critical infrastructure, though operating through consensus processes.

Highly influential within the copyright/IP domain — filed extensive Copyright Office comments, submitted AI Action Plan comments to Trump OSTP (Oct 2025), coordinated litigation (Cohere suit, Feb 2025), and brokered collective licensing deals (Prorata, 500+ publishers); influence is domain-limited to copyright/IP, but AI copyright governance is unambiguously their top priority.

CDT maintains a dedicated Disability Rights & AI project; CDT and AAPD published "Building a Disability-Inclusive AI Ecosystem" (March 2025) covering employment, education, benefits, healthcare, criminal justice, and transportation AI. They influence policy through research and coalition advocacy — including op-eds against AI regulation moratoriums — but lack direct governance power.

scored.player: Gig Workers Rising / National Employment Law Project
scored.rationale: Advocate for algorithmic transparency in the gig economy, an issue gaining traction — HRW's "The Gig Trap" (May 2025) documented algorithmic wage exploitation across 7 major platforms, and Colorado SB 24-75 was directly influenced by worker-organized data collection via the FairFare tool; the EU Platform Work Directive now classifies gig worker AI as high-risk, but organizational governance influence remains at the advocacy and coalition level.

With **38M+ members** and significant lobbying power, AARP's Policy Book includes detailed AI governance positions on transparency, accountability, and third-party evaluation; partnered with OpenAI/OATS on AI literacy and scam prevention. However, AI governance is one of many priorities alongside healthcare, Social Security, and financial security.

Zero direct governance input — cannot vote, lobby, or participate in policy processes. COMPAS and similar tools are used in **46 US states** for pretrial, sentencing, and parole decisions with documented racial bias. Advocacy comes entirely through proxies (ACLU, EPIC, Vera Institute), which are actively engaged, but the population itself is powerless.

Virtually no direct governance influence despite being heavily surveilled — DHS deployed **105 AI use cases** in immigration (2024 inventory), including ICE's Mobile Fortify (100,000+ uses) and Palantir's ImmigrationOS ($30M contract). Proxy advocates are highly active: American Immigration Council, Mijente ("Automating Deportation" report), and Sen. Markey introduced legislation to ban DHS facial recognition.

Minimal direct governance influence. TechTonic Justice's "Inescapable AI" report found essentially all **92 million low-income Americans** are exposed to AI decision-making across housing (16.3M households), employment (32.4M workers), and SNAP (42M people). Michigan's automated fraud system falsely accused 48,000 recipients. Proxy advocacy exists (TechTonic Justice, Benefits Tech Advocacy Hub) but is concentrated among few organizations.

Deeply affected — **$3.4B+** lost to AI-powered fraud in 2024, and STAT's Pulitzer-finalist "Denied by AI" exposed how Medicare Advantage plans use algorithms to deny care to 31M+ beneficiaries. CMS's WISeR model (launching Jan 2026) will use AI for Medicare prior authorizations. Direct governance power is limited; strongest proxy is AARP with moderate engagement.

Growing but limited governance influence. Cherokee Nation convened an AI/data sovereignty task force (late 2024); Māori-led Te Hiku Media built tikanga-governed language models; Indigenous AI Working Group published governance protocols. Frameworks like OCAP and CARE principles are increasingly cited, but Indigenous voices remain largely absent from mainstream AI governance forums.

Almost entirely absent from AI governance discussions despite ~60M Americans affected. Berkman Klein Center argued for a rural AI policy agenda (Oct 2023) and the RAISE AI Collaborative launched for rural schools (2025), but these are academic rather than community-driven. **17% lack broadband**, compounding exclusion. No dedicated rural AI governance organizations exist.

No direct governance voice — family court hearings are closed, records sealed, and families cannot learn their algorithmic risk scores. The Allegheny Family Screening Tool uses Medicaid, mental health, jail, and probate records to predict placement risk, with documented potential discrimination against Black families. Tools deployed in **26+ states**. DOJ Civil Rights Division is investigating, and ACLU published a detailed audit.

Minimal direct governance influence as one of the most vulnerable populations. AI chatbots proliferate as quasi-therapy tools; a 14-year-old died by suicide in 2024 after obsessive interaction with Character.AI. States are responding: **143 bills introduced** (Jan 2022–May 2025), with 11 states enacting 20 laws, but legislative action comes from lawmakers and advocates, not from the affected population.
